prompt: It can reasonably be inferred from the passage that the octopus's color change is

choices.text: produced by muscles widening sacs of pigment beneath the skin
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage states muscles widen the sacs to reveal color.

choices.text: triggered by the temperature of the surrounding seawater
choices.rationale: Temperature is never mentioned as a cause.

choices.text: perceptible to the octopus itself but not to other animals
choices.rationale: The octopus is colorblind, so it cannot perceive its own colors.

choices.text: learned only gradually over the course of the animal's life
choices.rationale: The change happens in under a second, not slowly over a lifetime.

explanation: The text directly ties color change to muscles pulling on pigment sacs.

stimulus.title: The Color of Thought
stimulus.text: An octopus has no bones and, oddly, almost no color of its own. Yet in less than a second it can flush deep red with alarm or fade to match the speckled gray of a stone. Beneath its skin lie millions of tiny sacs of pigment, each ringed by muscle. When the muscles pull, the sacs widen and color blooms; when they relax, the color vanishes. Stranger still, the octopus is colorblind, so it cannot see the very hues it produces. Some researchers suspect its skin itself senses light, letting the animal paint a disguise it can never admire.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that a 'clean line' on a finished map represents

choices.text: a once-open question that exploration eventually answered
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says every clean line 'was once a question,' settled by explorers.

choices.text: a mistake that no mapmaker ever got around to correcting
choices.rationale: The line marks a settled answer, not an uncorrected mistake.

choices.text: a region that, even now, remains completely unknown
choices.rationale: Clean lines mark known, corrected regions.

choices.text: a deliberate decoration much like a painted sea monster
choices.rationale: Clean lines replaced decorations, not added them.

explanation: A clean line stands for a once-open question now resolved.

stimulus.title: The Edge of the Map
stimulus.text: Old maps are full of guesses. Where knowledge ran out, mapmakers filled the blank spaces with mountains they had never seen and coastlines that bent the wrong way. Some added sea monsters, less from belief than from a need to decorate the unknown. As explorers returned with measurements, the guesses were slowly corrected, and the monsters swam off the page. But a finished, accurate map can hide how hard it was to make. Every clean line was once a question, settled only by someone willing to sail past the edge of what was known and come back with an answer.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the first version of the letter was

choices.text: an honest account of his deep homesickness
choices.rationale: The honest account was the second version, not the first.

choices.text: written to confess how far from home he felt
choices.rationale: Confessing his homesickness describes the later realization, not the cheerful first draft.

choices.text: more cheerful than Tomas actually felt
choices.rationale: Correct. He was cheerful 'as if homesickness were a thing he had outgrown,' though he had not.

choices.text: the version he ultimately decided to send
choices.rationale: He sent none of the versions.

explanation: The first draft put on cheerfulness he did not truly feel.

stimulus.title: The Unsent Letter
stimulus.text: Tomas wrote the letter three times before he stopped trying to send it. In the first version he was cheerful, listing the small triumphs of his new city as if homesickness were a thing he had outgrown. In the second he was honest, and the page filled with everything he missed until it embarrassed him. The third version said almost nothing at all. He kept that one folded in a drawer. Months later he found it again and realized the words he could not send had still done their work: writing them had let him admit, if only to himself, how far from home he truly felt.

prompt: The comparison to a fire extinguisher mainly suggests that the vault is

choices.text: dangerous and therefore risky to keep anywhere nearby
choices.rationale: No danger is implied.

choices.text: useful for nothing other than literally putting out fires
choices.rationale: The analogy is about readiness, not literal firefighting.

choices.text: valuable precisely because it is ready even if it is rarely used
choices.rationale: Correct. Like an extinguisher, its worth lies in readiness for a rare emergency.

choices.text: drawn upon constantly throughout every month of the year
choices.rationale: The passage hopes it is rarely needed.

explanation: The analogy stresses preparedness for an uncommon emergency.

stimulus.title: A Vault for Seeds
stimulus.text: Deep inside a frozen mountain, a vault holds something more valuable than gold: seeds. Hundreds of thousands of varieties of crops, gathered from around the world, rest in sealed packets at temperatures far below freezing. The cold slows the seeds' aging to a crawl, so that even after decades they can still sprout. The vault is a backup. If a war, flood, or disease wipes out a crop somewhere, a sample can be withdrawn and grown again. Its keepers hope it will rarely be needed, the way one hopes never to use a fire extinguisher. Its value lies precisely in being ready and almost never used.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that, before automation, a lighthouse's reliability depended on

choices.text: electric lamps, timers, and remote sensors doing the work
choices.rationale: Timers and sensors came later, with automation.

choices.text: the constant nightly labor of a dedicated human keeper
choices.rationale: Correct. Keepers performed the nightly tasks that kept the light burning.

choices.text: the sheer height of the tower above the surrounding water
choices.rationale: Height alone did not keep the light lit.

choices.text: the complete absence of storms along that stretch of coast
choices.rationale: Storms occurred and made the work dangerous.

explanation: Reliability rested on the keeper's constant labor.

stimulus.title: Keeping the Light
stimulus.text: For centuries, a lighthouse meant a keeper. Someone had to climb the tower each night, trim the wick, polish the lens, and wind the clockwork that turned the light. Storms made the work dangerous, and isolation made it lonely, but ships depended on that faithful beam. In the twentieth century, automation arrived. Electric lamps, timers, and remote sensors took over the tasks one by one, and keepers were gradually withdrawn. The lights still shine, more reliably than ever, yet something human slipped away with the last keeper. The machines kept the promise of the light; they could not keep its company.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that Nadia almost refused the loaner because she

choices.text: felt unworthy of playing such a fine and valuable instrument
choices.rationale: Correct. 'It felt wrong to play something so fine' signals a sense of unworthiness.

choices.text: had never learned how to read the music set before her
choices.rationale: Nothing suggests she cannot read music.

choices.text: strongly disliked the pieces she was assigned for the recital
choices.rationale: Her feelings about the pieces are not the reason given.

choices.text: actually preferred instruments whose tuning pegs kept slipping
choices.rationale: She struggled with slipping pegs; she did not prefer them.

explanation: Her hesitation stems from feeling the instrument was too fine for her.

stimulus.title: The Borrowed Violin
stimulus.text: Nadia had practiced on a school instrument so battered that two of its pegs slipped during every lesson. When her teacher handed her a loaner violin for the recital, she nearly refused; it felt wrong to play something so fine. But as she drew the bow across the strings, the tone opened like a window onto a bright street. Notes she had fought for all year now arrived easily, as if the instrument had been waiting for her. Afterward, returning it to its case, she understood that her old struggles had not been wasted. The fine violin had simply revealed the player she had quietly become.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that mixing glass colors during recycling

choices.text: has no real effect of any kind on the recycling process
choices.rationale: Mixing clearly has a negative effect.

choices.text: reduces the usefulness, and so the value, of the recycled material
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says mixing colors lowers the value of recycled glass.

choices.text: improves the overall strength of the new bottles that result
choices.rationale: Mixing lowers value; it does not improve strength.

choices.text: is actually required by most existing recycling regulations
choices.rationale: Sorting is recommended, not color mixing.

explanation: Mixed colors lower the recycled material's value, the passage states.

stimulus.title: The Endless Material
stimulus.text: Glass has a quiet superpower: it can be melted down and remade again and again without losing quality. A bottle recycled today can become a bottle next month, then another after that, in a loop that, in principle, never wears out. Compared with this ideal, reality lags. Much glass still ends up in landfills because collection is uneven and because mixing colors lowers the value of the recycled material. Sorting by color and keeping glass out of general trash would let more of it re-enter the loop. The material is willing; it is our systems, the author suggests, that need redesigning.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that draining and filling marshes most likely left coastal communities

choices.text: better protected from rising seas
choices.rationale: Draining removed the buffer, reducing protection from the sea.

choices.text: wealthier from the carbon they stored
choices.rationale: Draining released, rather than profited from, stored carbon.

choices.text: more exposed to the force of storms
choices.rationale: Correct. Since marshes blunt storms, removing them would increase exposure.

choices.text: surrounded by thicker marsh grasses
choices.rationale: Filling marshes destroys grasses rather than thickening them.

explanation: Removing the marsh buffer would leave communities more exposed to storms.

stimulus.title: The Quiet Buffer
stimulus.text: Where the land meets the sea, a salt marsh can look like wasteland: flat, muddy, and crossed by winding channels. Yet this drab landscape does remarkable work. Its dense grasses slow incoming waves, blunting the force of storms before they reach homes inland. The same grasses trap sediment, letting the marsh build itself upward as the sea slowly rises. Beneath the mud, roots store carbon that might otherwise warm the air. For years, marshes were drained and filled without much thought. Only later did people recognize that the unglamorous marsh had been protecting them all along, asking nothing in return.
